revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23984597483|manual triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:普天満宮]][[zh:普天滿宮]]
{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Futenma Shrine | image = File:Futemma-gū haiden.jpeg | location = 1-27-10 Futenma, [[:en:Ginowan|Ginowan]], {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|qid=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture|1=Okinawa Prefecture}}, Japan | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 17 | type seconds = 34.4 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 46 | longitude seconds = 37.9 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Okinawa Island | deity = {{ill|Kumano Gongen|qid=Q11568925|1=Kumano Gongen}} | shrine Rank = Former [[unranked shrine]] | founded = Unknown | main hall = [[Irimoya-zukuri]] | other names = Futenman Gongen | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = Lunar calendar: {{ill|September 15|en|September 15|qid=Q2846|lt=September 15|1=September 15}} | rituals = | location = {{ill|Futenma|qid=Q11514557|1=Futenma}} | coordinates = {{Coord|26.292917|N|127.777222|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | part_of = {{ill|Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū|qid=Q11573652|1=Eight Ryukyu Shrines}} | architecture_style = {{ill|East Asian hip-and-gable roof|qid=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}} }}'''Futenma Shrine''' (普天満宮, Futenma-gū) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in [[:en:Futenma|Futenma]], [[:en:Ginowan|Ginowan]], {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|qid=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture|1=Okinawa Prefecture}}, Japan. It is the only shrine in Ginowan City.
The official name is now '''Futenma Shrine''', though it is often referred to by its old name "Futenma Shrine". Another name is '''Futenman Gongen'''. It is commonly called '''Futenma Jingu'''. It is one of the [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines|Ryukyu Eight Shrines]].
== Deities == The shrine enshrines deities from ancient Ryukyu Shinto and [[Kumano Gongen]]. According to the ''{{ill|Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|en|Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|qid=Q11573685|lt=Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|1=Ryūkyū Shintō-ki}}'' (1648), "The seven major shrines of this country are dedicated to the Kumano Gongen and one to Hachiman Daibosatsu." About Futenman Gongen, it states, "Its origin is unknown, but it appears as the flying dragon of Kumano. To the east, there is a waterfall, which is the sacred water." ;Kumano Gongen *{{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|qid=Q682306|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto|1=Izanami}} *[[Kumano Gongen|Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] *[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] *[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-Omikami]] *Ketomiko deity ;Ryukyu ancient Shinto deities *Sun deity *Dragon palace deity ([[Niraikanai]] deity) *Futenma goddess (Guji deity) *Heaven deity *Earth deity *Sea deity
== History == The origin of the shrine dates back to ancient times when Ryukyu Shinto deities were worshiped in the cave (now known as [[Futenma Shrine Cave]]). During the reign of {{ill|Shō Kinpuku|en|Shō Kinpuku|qid=Q1060307|lt=King Shō Kinpuku|1=Shō Kinpuku}} to {{ill|Shō Taikyū|en|Shō Taikyū|qid=Q1139919|lt=King Shō Taikyū|1=Shō Taikyū}} (mid-15th century), [[Kumano Gongen]] was enshrined together. The oldest existing record is the "Futenman Gongen Monument" from 1590 (Wanli 18), and references to the shrine can be found in the ''{{ill|Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|en|Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|qid=Q11573685|lt=Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|1=Ryūkyū Shintō-ki}}'' and ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' (1713), as well as ''[[Ryukyu Kingdom Old Records]]'' (1731)<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=御由緒 {{!}} 普天満宮とは {{!}} 普天満宮 公式サイト |url=http://futenmagu.or.jp/about/history/ |website=futenmagu.or.jp |access-date=2022-08-30}}</ref>.
Futenma Shrine was regarded as the flying dragon Gongen of the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrines<ref>The other two mountains were represented by [[Suekichi Shrine]] as [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Shingu]] and [[Shikina-gū]] as [[Kumano Hongū Taisha|Hongu]] in Naha.</ref>. The shrine was also part of the widespread [[Kumano faith]] in early modern Okinawa, including sub-shrines and sites blending with local beliefs such as [[Bijuru]], [[Kannon]], and sacred stones, with many {{ill|Haisho|ja|拝所|lt=worship sites|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haisho|qq=}} across the prefecture. According to legend, a goddess appeared in the cave at Futenma after first appearing in {{ill|Shuri|en|Shuri|qid=Q1687821|lt=Shuri|1=Shuri}}, then reappeared as a hermit, declaring, "I am Kumano Gongen." Another legend tells of a peasant couple in [[Nakagusuku]] and {{ill|Misato|en|Misato|qid=Q420177|lt=Misato|1=Misato}} receiving golden blessings from the cave deity, which led to a tradition of pilgrimage in gratitude. The lunar calendar's September pilgrimage to Futenma was once attended by the [[King of Chuzan]], {{ill|Noro|en|Noro|qid=Q1902320|lt=Noro|1=Noro}}, and common people from all over<ref>From the history board at Futenma Shrine.</ref>. {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}}
== Meiji == After the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|en|Invasion of Ryukyu|qid=Q1140134|lt=Invasion of Ryukyu|1=Invasion of Ryukyu}} in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|en|Kikoe-ōgimi|qid=Q11611193|lt=Kikoe-ōgimi|1=Kikoe-ōgimi}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|Shō Hō|en|Shō Hō|qid=Q1041365|lt=1625|1=Shō Hō}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|Shō Ken|en|Shō Ken|qid=Q1061074|lt=New|1=Shō Ken}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|en|Shō Shōken|qid=Q843010|lt=Shō Shōken|1=Shō Shōken}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|1=Eight Ryukyu Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11573652}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|en|Naminoue Shrine|qid=Q704636|lt=Naminoue Shrine|1=Naminoue Shrine}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}}, {{ill|Hachiman shrine|en|Hachiman shrine|qid=Q11390939|lt=Asato-Hachimangū‎|1=Hachiman shrine}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikina-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chojugu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (later {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukishima Shrine|qq=}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} (1970 aerial photo)]]
== Okinawa War and Aftermath == During the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|qid=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa|1=Battle of Okinawa}} in 1945, the shrine priest evacuated with the deities to southern Okinawa, to [[:en:Itoman|Itoman]]. After the war, the shrine site was part of the US military base {{ill|Camp Foster|en|Camp Foster|qid=Q4419750|lt=Camp Foster|1=Camp Foster}} and was off-limits. A temporary shrine was built in the priest's hometown, [[:en:Gushikawa|Gushikawa]] (now part of {{ill|Uruma|en|Uruma|qid=Q694313|lt=Uruma|1=Uruma}})<ref name=":0" />.
In 1949, the US military released the site, and the shrine was moved back. Initially, there were only simple torii and buildings<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=「普天間」から「普天満」へ 映像でたどる神社の変遷【古写真から読みとく当時の街の姿 Okinawaタイムマシーン航時機】 |url=https://ryukyushimpo.jp/style/living-style/entry-1424928.html |website=琉球新報Style |access-date=2022-08-30 |language=ja}}</ref>.
In 1953, reconstruction began with support from Okinawan communities in Hawaii and local and external donations.
On May 15, 1972, upon the reversion of Okinawa to Japan, Futenma Shrine obtained legal status as a religious corporation and became affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]<ref name=":0" />.
== Futenma Shrine Cave == Behind the main hall is the [[Futenma Shrine Cave]], a 280-meter-long limestone cave. Artifacts from the later half of the early Okinawan shell mound period (about 3,000 years ago) and animal fossils from 20,000 years ago have been excavated here. It was designated as a scenic spot by Ginowan City on March 1, 1991, and a portion is open to the public for free<ref name=":0" />.
=== Inner Shrine === The cave, known as [[Futenma Shrine Cave]], houses an inner shrine. It is open to visitors from 10 am to 5 pm (with exceptions during New Year’s holidays and other shrine events). One of the sacred stones in the inner shrine, called the "Kohitsuji rock," is known for blessings in matchmaking<ref>[https://spicomi.net/media/articles/2854 Detailed report on weddings and shrine ceremonies at Futenma Shrine]</ref>.
== Futenma Pilgrimage Path ==
=== Ginowan Namimatsu === Along the Futenma pilgrimage path to Shuri was the [[Ginowan Namimatsu]], a 5km avenue of [[Ryukyu pine]]s, designated a National Natural Monument in 1932. The trees were mostly cut down for materials just before the war, and the rest were destroyed in battle. Today, the path is mostly within the US military base, with a few remaining pines lost to pests like the [[:en:Pine wilt nematode|Pine wilt nematode]].
=== Nakagusuku Kata West Road and Futenma Pilgrimage Path === In 1644, King {{ill|Shō Ken|en|Shō Ken|qid=Q1061074|lt=Shō Ken|1=Shō Ken}} began the annual September pilgrimage to Futenma Shrine to pray for good health, paving the pilgrimage path with stones and bridges. Some of the stone path and bridges remain in [[Urasoe Grand Park]]. However, most of the path is within the US base, only traceable from Shuri to Samashita Park. In 2012, the Nakagusuku Kata West Road and Futenma Pilgrimage Path, organized by the Ryukyu Kingdom, were designated as Historic Sites and Scenic Spots<ref>{{Cite web|title=Nakagusuku Kata West Road and Futenma Pilgrimage Path Cultural Heritage Online |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/207017 |website=bunka.nii.ac.jp |access-date=2022-08-30 |language=ja}}</ref>.
=== West Futenma's "Gun Road" === In 2018, during development of the returned land of West Futenma Residential Area from Camp Foster, a well-preserved section of the old "Futenma Old Road" was discovered<ref>{{Cite web|title=Remnants of the "Gun Road" discovered in West Futenma during development |url=https://ryukyushimpo.jp/news/entry-666874.html |website=琉球新報デジタル |access-date=2022-08-30 |language=ja}}</ref>.
== name == In the 1932 documentary "Actual Conditions of Famous Places and Historic Sites in Okinawa Prefecture," the torii inscription reads "Futenma Shrine." The rebuilt torii in 1963 reads "Futenma Shrine"<ref name=":1" />. From 2005 to 2006, during reconstruction of the main hall and shrine office, the name was officially changed from "Futenma Shrine" to "Futenma Shrine"<ref>Junhito Kaji, "Shrines of Okinawa," Okinawa Library (2019/7/31) No. 1527</ref>.
== festivals == * Main festival: Lunar calendar September 15
== Timeline == * Mid-15th century: Kumano Gongen enshrined during King Shō Kinpuku to King Shō Taikyū's reign. * March 1868: [[Shinbutsu bunri]] decree. * May 14, 1871: Modern shrine Ranking system established. * 1945: Destroyed in the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|qid=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa|1=Battle of Okinawa}}. * December 15, 1945: [[Shinto Directive]] issued. * 1953: Inner shrine rebuilt. * 1960: Futenma Shrine Support Association established. * 1963: Haiden rebuilt. * 1968: Main hall rebuilt. * 1968-1969: Kumano Gongen re-enshrined from Kumano Sanzan. * December 18, 1973: Registered as a religious corporation. * April 3, 2004: Temporary relocation for rebuilding of haiden and main hall.
== Access == {{location map|Japan mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|26.292917|127.777222}}|caption=Futenma Shrine|width=256}} === Bus === {{main|Okinawa Island bus routes}} '''Futenma Bus Stop''' (2-5 minutes on foot) * Route 22 - Children's Kingdom Miyazato Line ({{ill|Ryukyu Bus Kotsu|en|Ryukyu Bus Kotsu|qid=Q11573640|lt=Ryukyu Bus Kotsu|1=Ryukyu Bus Kotsu}}) * Route 23 - Gushikawa Line (Ryukyu Bus Kotsu) * Route 25 - Futenma Airport Line ([[Naha Bus]]) * Route 27 - Yakena (Ojana) Line (Ryukyu Bus Kotsu and [[Okinawa Bus]]) * Route 31 - Awase West Line ({{ill|Toyo Bus|en|Toyo Bus|qid=Q11528673|lt=Toyo Bus|1=Toyo Bus}}) * Route 52 - Yosha Line (Okinawa Bus) * Route 58 - Baten Ryudai Awase Line (Toyo Bus) * Route 61 - Maehara Line (Okinawa Bus) * Route 77 - Nago East (Henoko) Line (Okinawa Bus) * Route 80 - Yonashiro Line (Okinawa Bus) * Route 88 - Ginowan Line (Ryukyu Bus Kotsu) * Route 90 - Chibana (Bypass) Line (Ryukyu Bus Kotsu) * Route 223 - Gushikawa Omoromachi Line (Ryukyu Bus Kotsu) * Route 227 - Yakena Omoromachi Line (Ryukyu Bus Kotsu and Okinawa Bus) * Route 290 - Chibana Omoromachi Line (Ryukyu Bus Kotsu) :Reference: [http://www.kotsu-okinawa.org Bus map Okinawa]
== References == {{References}} {{reflist|2}} <references group="Note" /><references />
== Related Pages == *[[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] *{{ill|Harukana Receive|en|Harukana Receive|qid=Q33132491|lt=Harukana Receive|1=Harukana Receive}} - Features Ginowan Shrine, modeled after Futenma Shrine.
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Futemma-gū}} * [http://futenmagu.or.jp/ Official Site] * [http://rca.open.ed.jp/city-2002/road/history/his3_040_t.html Futenma Road Opening] (Okinawa Prefectural Comprehensive Education Center, "Ryukyu Culture Archive") {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}}
{{commonscat|Futemma-gū}}
{{Okinawan Shrines}}
[[File:Futemma-gū haiden.jpeg|270px]] [[File:普天満宮 (1970年の空中写真).png|thumb|244x244px|Futenma Shrine within the US military base {{ill|Camp Foster|en|Camp Foster|qid=Q4419750|lt=Camp Foster|1=Camp Foster}} [[File:Futenma-gū Cave.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Inner shrine]] [[File:Futenma-gū Honden.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Main hall]] [[File:Futemma-gū entrance.jpeg|250px|right|thumb|Torii]] {{translated page|ja|普天満宮|version=101708144|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:普天満宮]]
[[zh:%E6%99%AE%E5%A4%A9%E6%BB%BF%E5%AE%AE]]
